name: Banshee
description: The Banshee, derived from the Irish bean sí (woman of the sídhe or fairy mound), is a female spirit in Irish folklore whose wail foretells an impending death. Primarily associated with specific Irish families, particularly those with 'O' or 'Mac' prefixes in their surnames, the Banshee's appearance and behavior vary depending on regional traditions. She is not a harbinger of death itself, but rather a messenger, lamenting the loss of a family member. The intensity of her cry is said to correlate with the status and importance of the deceased. While sometimes described as a solitary figure, multiple Banshees can appear, signaling the death of someone very important or holy. Sightings are considered extremely rare, and encounters are treated with grave respect and fear. She is a powerful symbol of grief, fate, and the deep connection between the living and the spirit world in Irish culture.
subtitle: A wailing spirit foretelling death.
appearance: Often depicted as a gaunt, pale woman with long, flowing hair, sometimes red or white. She may wear a grey cloak or a white winding sheet. In some accounts, she appears as an old woman or a beautiful young woman, depending on the family and the circumstances.
habitat: Typically found near the homes of the families she is associated with, often in rural areas, near bodies of water, or ancient burial grounds. She might also be present in places associated with death and mourning.
behavior: The Banshee is primarily known for her wailing. She is said to appear or be heard before a death, often keening (a traditional Irish lament). Her behavior is driven by grief and a deep connection to the families she watches over. She is not typically malevolent, but her presence is always a sign of sorrow.

keyMyths.title: The Wail of the O'Malley Clan
keyMyths.description: Legend tells of a Banshee who always appeared before the death of an O'Malley clan chieftain. Her wail was so piercing that it could be heard for miles around, a clear indication of the chieftain's impending demise. It is said that to hear her wail was to know death was near, and prepare for the inevitable.

keyMyths.title: The River Banshee
keyMyths.description: A story tells of a Banshee who haunts a specific river, wailing whenever someone from her associated family is about to drown. Locals avoid swimming in that river after dark, fearing to provoke her mournful cry. The river is said to be cursed by the spirit's grief.

keyMyths.title: The Banshee's Comb
keyMyths.description: It is said that if one finds a Banshee's comb, a silver comb left behind by the spirit near a stream, one must never pick it up. To do so invites the Banshee's wrath, and she will haunt the offender until they return the comb to its rightful place, or until their own death.

keyMyths.title: The Bean Nighe
keyMyths.description: In some regions of Scotland, a similar figure known as the Bean Nighe (washing woman) is described as a banshee-like spirit who is seen washing the bloodstained clothes of those about to die. This figure is often seen as a harbinger of doom and a symbol of impending tragedy.

sources.author: Patricia Lysaght
sources.work: The Banshee: The Irish Death Messenger
sources.description: An in-depth academic study of the Banshee in Irish folklore, examining its origins, variations, and cultural significance.
sources.text: An in-depth academic study of the Banshee in Irish folklore, examining its origins, variations, and cultural significance.

sources.author: W.B. Yeats
sources.work: Fairy and Folk Tales of the Irish Peasantry
sources.description: A collection of Irish folklore, including several stories and descriptions of Banshees, providing insights into their role in Irish society.
sources.text: A collection of Irish folklore, including several stories and descriptions of Banshees, providing insights into their role in Irish society.

sources.author: Lady Wilde
sources.work: Ancient Legends, Mystic Charms, and Superstitions of Ireland
sources.description: A compilation of Irish legends and superstitions, offering a glimpse into the beliefs surrounding the Banshee and other supernatural beings.
sources.text: A compilation of Irish legends and superstitions, offering a glimpse into the beliefs surrounding the Banshee and other supernatural beings.

sources.author: Peter Narváez
sources.work: The Good People: New Fairylore Essays
sources.description: Essays exploring the persistence of fairy beliefs, including the Banshee, in modern Irish culture.
sources.text: Essays exploring the persistence of fairy beliefs, including the Banshee, in modern Irish culture.
